skill.name: esm
skill.description: Toolkit guidance for using ESM3, ESM C, and Forge to generate protein sequences, predict structures, create embeddings, and run protein design workflows.
skill.summary: Use ESM models for protein generation, structure prediction, embeddings, and research workflows.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many hits from Markdown examples, including false positives for code fences, model.eval(), and MD5 cache keys. No prompt injection or malicious exfiltration evidence was found, but the skill has medium risk because it enables dual-use protein design and documents remote Forge API use with local file caches.

medium_findings.title: Dual-use protein generation guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill gives practical workflows for novel protein design, function-conditioned generation, inverse folding, and variant libraries. This is legitimate research guidance, but generated protein designs can have biosafety implications and need expert review before experimental use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections explicitly describe generating novel proteins, conditioning on function, inverse folding, and optimizing sequences. The content also includes responsible-use language, so this is dual-use research risk rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: External Forge API inference with token use
medium_findings.description: The skill documents sending protein inputs to EvolutionaryScale Forge with an API token. This can expose proprietary sequences or research data to an external service if users do not apply data governance controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The remote client and token placeholder are clearly documented, with hardcoded Forge URLs. No evidence shows credential exfiltration or malicious endpoints.

low_findings.title: Local result files and pickle cache examples
low_findings.description: Several examples write generated sequences, PDB files, checkpoints, and caches to local files. The cache examples use pickle loading, which is unsafe for untrusted files and should be limited to trusted local data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes and pickle operations are directly shown in examples. They are not hidden behavior, but users could mishandle untrusted cache files.

low_findings.title: Shell installation commands in documentation
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many Markdown code fences as external command execution. Confirmed command examples are ordinary package installation steps and are not executed by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are fenced documentation examples for installing the ESM package. There is no wrapper script or automatic execution path in the skill files.

low_findings.title: Static eval finding is a false positive
low_findings.description: The reported eval pattern refers to PyTorch model evaluation mode, not dynamic code execution. No evidence found of Python eval, JavaScript eval, or user-controlled code execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context shows esm_model.eval(), a standard neural network inference call. It does not evaluate source code or user-supplied strings.

low_findings.title: MD5 used only for cache keys
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged weak cryptography. The reviewed uses generate non-security cache filenames from protein inputs and are not used for authentication, signatures, or encryption.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed MD5 calls hash local cache input data only. Weak collision resistance is not a credential or integrity control in these examples.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented remote inference endpoint
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes a hardcoded Forge endpoint for cloud inference. This is expected for Forge usage, but users should avoid sending restricted sequences without approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint is visibly documented as the vendor service for model inference. No evidence found of covert networking or unexpected domains.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pickle cache loading in examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples load pickle files for checkpoints and caches. Pickle can execute code when loading malicious files, so these examples should only load trusted local artifacts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pickle.load calls are explicit and are a real unsafe pattern for untrusted files. The examples use local cache files, which lowers the severity.

content.value_statement: Protein model work requires careful model selection, sequence handling, and validation. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured ESM workflows for research use.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use ESM3, ESM C, and Forge API models.
Provides examples for sequence generation, completion, and inverse folding.
Shows structure prediction and PDB export workflows.
Guides embedding generation for clustering and downstream models.
Documents batch processing, caching, and retry patterns for Forge.
Includes responsible-use reminders for protein design work.
limitations: Does not validate biological safety or experimental feasibility by itself.
Requires the external ESM package and suitable hardware or Forge access.
Forge workflows may send sequence data to an external service.
Examples need adaptation for real datasets, controls, and lab protocols.

use_cases.title: Prototype protein design workflows
use_cases.description: Plan ESM3 prompts for sequence completion, structure generation, and iterative refinement before running experiments.

use_cases.title: Create embedding analysis pipelines
use_cases.description: Use ESM C embeddings for clustering, similarity analysis, feature extraction, and property prediction workflows.

use_cases.title: Scale inference with Forge
use_cases.description: Design batch processing, checkpointing, caching, and retry patterns for cloud-based ESM inference.

prompt_templates.title: Choose an ESM model
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose between ESM3, ESM C, and Forge for my protein analysis task. My goal is [describe goal].

prompt_templates.title: Plan sequence generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft an ESM3 workflow to generate protein sequence candidates from this starting context: [sequence, structure, or function]. Include validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Build an embedding pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a workflow for generating ESM C embeddings from my protein dataset and clustering the results. Include batching and storage guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Forge deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Forge API inference plan for data handling, retries, rate limits, caching, and biosafety review checkpoints.

output_examples.input: User asks which ESM model to use for a small sequence similarity project.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends ESM C 300M for quick embeddings, notes when 600M may improve quality, and suggests caching repeated results.

output_examples.input: User asks for a workflow to design variants around a known protein sequence.
output_examples.output: The skill outlines masked sequence generation, structure checks, diversity screening, and a reminder to complete biosafety review before lab work.

output_examples.input: User asks how to process many proteins with Forge.
output_examples.output: The skill proposes batching, retry handling, checkpoint files, rate-limit awareness, and controls for sensitive sequence data.

best_practices: Start with smaller local models for prototypes before using larger Forge models.
Keep proprietary sequences out of external services unless data sharing is approved.
Validate generated designs with domain experts and appropriate biosafety review.
anti_patterns: Do not treat generated protein sequences as experimentally validated designs.
Do not load pickle cache files from untrusted sources.
Do not send restricted biological data to Forge without approval.

faq.question: What models does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers ESM3 for generation, ESM C for embeddings, and Forge for cloud inference.

faq.question: Can it run ESM models directly?
faq.answer: The skill provides guidance and examples. Actual execution requires installing the ESM package and model access.

faq.question: Does it require a GPU?
faq.answer: Local large-model workflows usually benefit from a GPU. Forge can run remote inference without local GPU hardware.

faq.question: Is Forge required?
faq.answer: No. Some smaller models can run locally, while larger production models require Forge access.

faq.question: Can it validate biological safety?
faq.answer: No. It reminds users to involve qualified experts and formal biosafety review for generated designs.

faq.question: How should sensitive sequences be handled?
faq.answer: Keep sensitive sequences local unless external processing is approved by the relevant data owner.
